slides.title: Wi-Fi is a wireless networking technology used across the globe.
lines: Wi-Fi is a wireless networking technology used across the globe.
Wi-Fi refers to any system that uses the 802.11 standard, which was developed by the Institute of Electrical and Electronics Engineers (IEEE) and released in 1997.
The term Wi-Fi, which is alternatively spelled
, a trade group that pioneered commercialization of the technology.

slides.title: No inherent physical protection
lines: Physical connections between devices are replaced by logical associations 
Sending and receiving messages do not need physical access to the network infrastructure (cables, hubs, routers, etc.)
Why security is more of a concern in wireless?
slides.text:
No inherent physical protection
Physical connections between devices are replaced by logical associations 
Sending and receiving messages do not need physical access to the network infrastructure (cables, hubs, routers, etc.)
Why security is more of a concern in wireless?

slides.title: Broadcast communications 
lines: Wireless usually means radio, which has a broadcast nature
Transmissions can be overheard by anyone in range – anyone can generate transmissions,
which will be received by other devices in range
which will interfere with other nearby transmissions and may prevent their correct reception (jamming)
Why security is more of a concern in wireless?
slides.text:
Broadcast communications 
Wireless usually means radio, which has a broadcast nature
Transmissions can be overheard by anyone in range – anyone can generate transmissions,
which will be received by other devices in range
which will interfere with other nearby transmissions and may prevent their correct reception (jamming)
Why security is more of a concern in wireless?

slides.title: Eavesdropping is easy
lines: Injecting bogus messages into the network is easy
Replaying previously recorded messages is easy
Illegitimate access to the network and its services is easy
Denial of service is easily achieved by jamming
Why security is more of a concern in wireless?

lines: Easy to perform, most impossible to detect
By default , everything is transmitted in clear text
No security offered by the transmission medium
Different tools available on the internet
With the right equipment, it’s possible to eavesdrop traffic from few kilometers away
slides.text:

By default , everything is transmitted in clear text
Username, passwords, content...

slides.title: Attacker spoofs a disassociate message from the victim
lines: Attacker spoofs a disassociate message from the victim
The victim starts to look for a new AP, and the attacker advertises his own AP on a different channel, using the real AP’s MAC address
The attacker connects to the real AP using victim’s MAC address
slides.text:
Attacker spoofs a disassociate message from the victim
The victim starts to look for a new AP, and the attacker advertises his own AP on a different channel, using the real AP’s MAC address
The attacker connects to the real AP using victim’s MAC address
Man In The Middle Attack

lines: to achieve any of the above goals
slides.text:
WEP is Wired Equivalent Privacy
Link-layer encryption
Defined in the IEEE 802.11 standard
Goals of WEP
Confidentiality
Access control
Data integrity
Yet, WEP
failed
to achieve any of the above goals
So what is WEP?

slides.title: Uses RC4 (stream cipher) for encryption
lines: A compromise of hardware costs, speed, and security
slides.text:
Uses RC4 (stream cipher) for encryption
A compromise of hardware costs, speed, and security
Originally (1999) 64 bit (40 +24):
40 bit key
24 bit Initialization Vector (IV)
128 bit (104 + 14):
104 bit (13 ASCII characters, 26 Hex-Characters) key. 104 = 13*8.
24 bit Initialization Vector (IV)
256 bit:
232 bit key
Available, but not common
WEP Flavors

slides.title: Master keys are unlikely to be changed due to key management issues (more on this later…)
lines: Master keys are unlikely to be changed due to key management issues (more on this later…)
24 bits of IV are not sufficient to avoid collisions
Assuming 1500-byte packets at 11 Mbps, 2^24 possible IVs will be exhausted in about 5 hours
If IVs are assigned randomly, collisions can be expected (50% probability) after 5000 packets (Birthday paradox).
If IVs are assigned sequentially, collisions are inevitable if cards are re-initialized (e.g., always start from zero)
slides.text:
Master keys are unlikely to be changed due to key management issues (more on this later…)
24 bits of IV are not sufficient to avoid collisions
Assuming 1500-byte packets at 11 Mbps, 2^24 possible IVs will be exhausted in about 5 hours
If IVs are assigned randomly, collisions can be expected (50% probability) after 5000 packets (Birthday paradox).
If IVs are assigned sequentially, collisions are inevitable if cards are re-initialized (e.g., always start from zero)
Keystream Reuse/Collision

slides.title: It’s carried in plaintext in the “encrypted” message!
lines: It’s carried in plaintext in the “encrypted” message!
There are no restrictions on IV reuse!
The IV forms a significant portion of the “seed” for the RC4 algorithm!
It was discovered that it is easy to decrypt RC4
if part of the key is known.
slides.text:
It’s carried in plaintext in the “encrypted” message!
It’s only 24 bits!
There are no restrictions on IV reuse!
The IV forms a significant portion of the “seed” for the RC4 algorithm!
It was discovered that it is easy to decrypt RC4
ciphertext
if part of the key is known.
Initialization vector (IV)

slides.title: It has been known for a while that RC4 has weak keys
lines: It has been known for a while that RC4 has weak keys
Weak keys are more susceptible to showing the relationship between plaintext and
Weak IVs correspond to weak Keys.
Weak IVs have the following patterns:
There are approx. 9000 weak IVs out of the 40 bit WEP secret keys. It is about 5% of total number IVs.
Weak IVs and Weak Keys in RC4
slides.text:
It has been known for a while that RC4 has weak keys
Weak keys are more susceptible to showing the relationship between plaintext and
ciphertext
Weak IVs correspond to weak Keys.
Weak IVs have the following patterns:
First byte: range from 3 to 7
Second byte: 255
Third byte: anything
There are approx. 9000 weak IVs out of the 40 bit WEP secret keys. It is about 5% of total number IVs.
Weak IVs and Weak Keys in RC4

slides.title: The ICV is generated from a cyclic redundancy check (CRC-32)
lines: The ICV is generated from a cyclic redundancy check (CRC-32)
Can be done easily by anyone.
Easy for an attacker to change a packet and then change ICV to get response from AP.
E.g., replay attacks or injection attacks
slides.text:
The ICV is generated from a cyclic redundancy check (CRC-32)
Only a simple arithmetic computation.
Can be done easily by anyone.
Not cryptographically secure.
Easy for an attacker to change a packet and then change ICV to get response from AP.
E.g., replay attacks or injection attacks
Weak Message Integrity (ICV)

slides.title: WEP does
lines: Master keys could be one of four globally shared keys
Often, one key is relied on
slides.text:
WEP does
NOT
include any key management protocols!
Master keys could be one of four globally shared keys
Key must be updated manually
Often, one key is relied on
Key Management

slides.title: Keys are manually distributed
lines: Implications: often infrequently changed and easy to remember!
Key values can be directly set as hex data
ASCII string is converted into keying material
slides.text:
Keys are manually distributed
Keys are statically configured
Implications: often infrequently changed and easy to remember!
Key values can be directly set as hex data
Key generators provided for convenience
ASCII string is converted into keying material
Non-standard but in wide use
WEP (Re)Keying in Practice

slides.title: Authentication is optional
lines: WEP uses a “Shared Key Authentication” protocol to authenticate stations
Responder sends initiator random challenge text
Initiator sends responder encrypted challenge text
Responder signals successful completion of protocol
This is supposed to prove that the initiator knows the correct key
However, if a keystream is known, no key is necessary, so anybody can authenticate!
slides.text:
Authentication is optional
WEP uses a “Shared Key Authentication” protocol to authenticate stations
Initiator requests shared key authentication
Responder sends initiator random challenge text
Initiator sends responder encrypted challenge text
Responder signals successful completion of protocol
This is supposed to prove that the initiator knows the correct key
However, if a keystream is known, no key is necessary, so anybody can authenticate!
User Authentication

slides.title: IV (key) reuse due to limited IV space
lines: IV (key) reuse due to limited IV space
Lots of known plaintext in IP, ICMP, ARP, and etc.
Possible to recover some portion of the keystream
Can extend partially recovered keystream to the entire keystream via repeated probing/trying
Lack of IV replay protection (ARP replay attacks)
Possible to flip bits in real time, which is enabled by linearity of CRC32 (weak integrity check)
slides.text:
IV (key) reuse due to limited IV space
Known plaintext  attack
Lots of known plaintext in IP, ICMP, ARP, and etc.
Partial known plaintext attack
e.g., the IP header
Possible to recover some portion of the keystream
Can extend partially recovered keystream to the entire keystream via repeated probing/trying
Weak Integrity check (ICV)
Lack of IV replay protection (ARP replay attacks)
Possible to flip bits in real time, which is enabled by linearity of CRC32 (weak integrity check)
WEP Weakness Summary

slides.title: No key management or exchange framework defined
lines: No key management or exchange framework defined
Enables station to roam to a rogue AP
slides.text:
No key management or exchange framework defined
Loose authentication
Beacon messages are not authenticated
Enables station to roam to a rogue AP
Disassociate (also De-
auth
), re-associate messages not authenticated
Enables
DoS
attacks
WEP Weakness Summary (Cont’d)

slides.title: “Power” of the WEP relies in the difficulty of discovery of the secret key through a brute-force attack
lines: “Power” of the WEP relies in the difficulty of discovery of the secret key through a brute-force attack
“Dictionary attack” uses dictionary of keys, not all possible keys
Such attack requires less then a month for all keys
Common Attacks: The Brute Force / Dictionary Attack
slides.text:
“Power” of the WEP relies in the difficulty of discovery of the secret key through a brute-force attack
“Dictionary attack” uses dictionary of keys, not all possible keys
Such attack requires less then a month for all keys

slides.title: Capture 2 WEP encrypted packets
lines: Try to decrypt it using the captured IV and a potential key
Verify the key on the 2nd packet (optional)
slides.text:
Capture 2 WEP encrypted packets
Try to decrypt it using the captured IV and a potential key
Verify decrypted ICV (the CRC)
Verify the key on the 2nd packet (optional)
The Brute Force / Dictionary Attack:
Steps

lines: , Martin and Shamir found a class of RC4 keys called “weak keys”
If the first 2 bytes of enough key stream are known -> The RC4 key is discovered
The first 8 bytes of WEP packet is a known SNAP-SAP header
slides.text:
2001 –
Fluhrer
, Martin and Shamir found a class of RC4 keys called “weak keys”
If the first 2 bytes of enough key stream are known -> The RC4 key is discovered
The first 8 bytes of WEP packet is a known SNAP-SAP header
AirSnort
implements this attack
Recovers key after 20,000 packets = 11 seconds
Common Attacks: The FMS Attack

slides.title: Does not recover the key, it just reveals the message
lines: Does not recover the key, it just reveals the message
Truncate the last byte and try to guess one “value” for plaintext
Correct the checksum and send packet to AP
If guess is correct the AP will reply
Repeat until all bytes are decrypted
slides.text:
Does not recover the key, it just reveals the message
Exploits an ICV vulnerability
Steps:
Capture one packet
Truncate the last byte and try to guess one “value” for plaintext
Correct the checksum and send packet to AP
If guess is correct the AP will reply
Repeat until all bytes are decrypted
Common Attacks: The
C
hopchop
Attack

lines: Guess the value of the deleted byte and calculate new ICV
slides.text:
KoreK
C
hopchop
Attack Illustrated

ENC. ICV
Encrypted Data

Guess the value of the deleted byte and calculate new ICV

slides.title: Fragmentation means chopping a packet into smaller packets
lines: Fragmentation means chopping a packet into smaller packets
Increases the traffic but improves performance in the presence of noise
This feature can be used for breaking WEP!
slides.text:
Fragmentation means chopping a packet into smaller packets
Increases the traffic but improves performance in the presence of noise
802.11 and IP protocol allow fragmentation
802.11 allows for at most 16 fragments
This feature can be used for breaking WEP!
Common Attacks: The Fragmentation Attack

slides.title: During fragmentation, WEP encryption is performed independently on each individual fragment using the same IV.
lines: During fragmentation, WEP encryption is performed independently on each individual fragment using the same IV.
Possible to send multiple fragments (16) using the same key stream
ou know more about the plaintext than you think you know.
Almost all encrypted data packets have a LLC/SNAP header, 8 bytes
XOR gives 8 bytes of the key stream
At this point, arbitrary data of (8 − 4) · 16 = 64 bytes (4 byte CRC tag) may be sent
slides.text:
During fragmentation, WEP encryption is performed independently on each individual fragment using the same IV.
Possible to send multiple fragments (16) using the same key stream
Y
ou know more about the plaintext than you think you know.
Almost all encrypted data packets have a LLC/SNAP header, 8 bytes
XOR gives 8 bytes of the key stream
At this point, arbitrary data of (8 − 4) · 16 = 64 bytes (4 byte CRC tag) may be sent

slides.title: Increase the number of bytes used for encryption (“protects” against FMS attack)
lines: Increase the number of bytes used for encryption (“protects” against FMS attack)
Remove the weak IV - keystream re-use vulnerabilities
Extensible Authentication Protocol (EAP) – change often the WEP-key
Deploy Intrusion Detection Systems (IDS) to protect against injected traffic
Companies sell hardware using modified versions of the WEP protocol claiming to be secure
slides.text:
Increase the number of bytes used for encryption (“protects” against FMS attack)
Remove the weak IV - keystream re-use vulnerabilities
Prevent key re-use
Extensible Authentication Protocol (EAP) – change often the WEP-key
Deploy Intrusion Detection Systems (IDS) to protect against injected traffic
Companies sell hardware using modified versions of the WEP protocol claiming to be secure
Protecting WEP

slides.title: WEP has a long history of vulnerabilities and “fixes”
lines: WEP has a long history of vulnerabilities and “fixes”
WEP is a good example of how attacks evolve and mature over time
Attacks that a few years ago took days, now take minutes if the right tools are used
2005 WEP is officially declared deprecated by IEEE 802.11 committee
2008 WEP used by 30% of users in a US university
Today – too many old networks, some using WEP
WEP must be abandoned once and for all, rather than patch it yet again !!!
slides.text:
WEP has a long history of vulnerabilities and “fixes”
WEP is a good example of how attacks evolve and mature over time
Attacks that a few years ago took days, now take minutes if the right tools are used
2005 WEP is officially declared deprecated by IEEE 802.11 committee
2008 WEP used by 30% of users in a US university
Today – too many old networks, some using WEP
WEP must be abandoned once and for all, rather than patch it yet again !!!
Conclusions

full_text:
Lecture 9:
Wired Equivalent Privacy (WEP)
Instructor:
Kui
Ren
Wireless network and
ioT
security

Wi-Fi is a wireless networking technology used across the globe.
Wi-Fi refers to any system that uses the 802.11 standard, which was developed by the Institute of Electrical and Electronics Engineers (IEEE) and released in 1997.
The term Wi-Fi, which is alternatively spelled
WiFi
,
Wi-fi
,
Wifi
, or
wifi
, was pushed by the
Wi-Fi Alliance
, a trade group that pioneered commercialization of the technology.
Wi-Fi

802.11 is a set of standards for  wireless local area network (WLAN)  computer communication,  developed by the IEEE LAN/MAN  Standards Committee (IEEE 802) in  the 5 GHz and 2.4 GHz public  spectrum bands.
802.11

Sensitive Information over Wi-Fi

No inherent physical protection
Physical connections between devices are replaced by logical associations 
Sending and receiving messages do not need physical access to the network infrastructure (cables, hubs, routers, etc.)
Why security is more of a concern in wireless?

Broadcast communications 
Wireless usually means radio, which has a broadcast nature
Transmissions can be overheard by anyone in range – anyone can generate transmissions,
which will be received by other devices in range
which will interfere with other nearby transmissions and may prevent their correct reception (jamming)
Why security is more of a concern in wireless?

Eavesdropping is easy
Injecting bogus messages into the network is easy
Replaying previously recorded messages is easy
Illegitimate access to the network and its services is easy
Denial of service is easily achieved by jamming
Why security is more of a concern in wireless?

Wireless technology doesn’t remove any old security issue
Eavesdropping
Man-in-the-middle attacks
Denial of Service
Threats

Easy to perform, most impossible to detect
By default , everything is transmitted in clear text
Username, passwords, content...
No security offered by the transmission medium
Different tools available on the internet
Network sniffers, protocol analyzers
Password collectors
With the right equipment, it’s possible to eavesdrop traffic from few kilometers away
Eavesdropping

Attacker spoofs a disassociate message from the victim
The victim starts to look for a new AP, and the attacker advertises his own AP on a different channel, using the real AP’s MAC address
The attacker connects to the real AP using victim’s MAC address
Man In The Middle Attack

Attack on transmission regency used
Frequency jamming
Not very technical, but works
Attack on MAC layer
Spoofed disassociation messages
Can target on specific user
Attacks on higher layer protocol (TCP/IP protocol)
SYN Flooding
Denial of Service

Confidentiality
Authenticity
Replay detection
Integrity
Access control
Protection against jamming
Wireless Communication Security Requirements

Wired Equivalent Privacy (WEP)
Wi-Fi Protected Access (WPA)
Wi-Fi Protected Access II (WPA2)
Wi-Fi Security Protocols

WEP is Wired Equivalent Privacy
Link-layer encryption
Defined in the IEEE 802.11 standard
Goals of WEP
Confidentiality
Access control
Data integrity
Yet, WEP
failed
to achieve any of the above goals
So what is WEP?

Uses RC4 (stream cipher) for encryption
A compromise of hardware costs, speed, and security
Originally (1999) 64 bit (40 +24):
40 bit key
24 bit Initialization Vector (IV)
128 bit (104 + 14):
104 bit (13 ASCII characters, 26 Hex-Characters) key. 104 = 13*8.
24 bit Initialization Vector (IV)
256 bit:
232 bit key
Available, but not common
WEP Flavors

WEP

Message: What you’re encrypting
CRC: To verify the integrity of the message
Plaintext: The message + CRC
Initialization vector (IV): A 24-bit number which plays two roles that we’ll meet in a moment
Key: A 40 or 104-bit number which is used to build the keystream
Keystream: What is used to encrypt the plaintext
Ciphertext
: What we end up post-encryption

The Stream-Cipher-Breakdown

Master keys are unlikely to be changed due to key management issues (more on this later…)
24 bits of IV are not sufficient to avoid collisions
Assuming 1500-byte packets at 11 Mbps, 2^24 possible IVs will be exhausted in about 5 hours
If IVs are assigned randomly, collisions can be expected (50% probability) after 5000 packets (Birthday paradox).
If IVs are assigned sequentially, collisions are inevitable if cards are re-initialized (e.g., always start from zero)
Keystream Reuse/Collision

It’s carried in plaintext in the “encrypted” message!
It’s only 24 bits!
There are no restrictions on IV reuse!
The IV forms a significant portion of the “seed” for the RC4 algorithm!
It was discovered that it is easy to decrypt RC4
ciphertext
if part of the key is known.
Initialization vector (IV)

It has been known for a while that RC4 has weak keys
Weak keys are more susceptible to showing the relationship between plaintext and
ciphertext
Weak IVs correspond to weak Keys.
Weak IVs have the following patterns:
First byte: range from 3 to 7
Second byte: 255
Third byte: anything
There are approx. 9000 weak IVs out of the 40 bit WEP secret keys. It is about 5% of total number IVs.
Weak IVs and Weak Keys in RC4

The ICV is generated from a cyclic redundancy check (CRC-32)
Only a simple arithmetic computation.
Can be done easily by anyone.
Not cryptographically secure.
Easy for an attacker to change a packet and then change ICV to get response from AP.
E.g., replay attacks or injection attacks
Weak Message Integrity (ICV)

WEP does
NOT
include any key management protocols!
Master keys could be one of four globally shared keys
Key must be updated manually
Often, one key is relied on
Key Management

Keys are manually distributed
Keys are statically configured
Implications: often infrequently changed and easy to remember!
Key values can be directly set as hex data
Key generators provided for convenience
ASCII string is converted into keying material
Non-standard but in wide use
WEP (Re)Keying in Practice

Authentication is optional
WEP uses a “Shared Key Authentication” protocol to authenticate stations
Initiator requests shared key authentication
Responder sends initiator random challenge text
Initiator sends responder encrypted challenge text
Responder signals successful completion of protocol
This is supposed to prove that the initiator knows the correct key
However, if a keystream is known, no key is necessary, so anybody can authenticate!
User Authentication

IV (key) reuse due to limited IV space
Known plaintext  attack
Lots of known plaintext in IP, ICMP, ARP, and etc.
Partial known plaintext attack
e.g., the IP header
Possible to recover some portion of the keystream
Can extend partially recovered keystream to the entire keystream via repeated probing/trying
Weak Integrity check (ICV)
Lack of IV replay protection (ARP replay attacks)
Possible to flip bits in real time, which is enabled by linearity of CRC32 (weak integrity check)
WEP Weakness Summary

No key management or exchange framework defined
Loose authentication
Beacon messages are not authenticated
Enables station to roam to a rogue AP
Disassociate (also De-
auth
), re-associate messages not authenticated
Enables
DoS
attacks

Common Attacks on WEP

“Power” of the WEP relies in the difficulty of discovery of the secret key through a brute-force attack
“Dictionary attack” uses dictionary of keys, not all possible keys
Such attack requires less then a month for all keys
Common Attacks: The Brute Force / Dictionary Attack

Capture 2 WEP encrypted packets
Try to decrypt it using the captured IV and a potential key
Verify decrypted ICV (the CRC)
Verify the key on the 2nd packet (optional)
The Brute Force / Dictionary Attack:
Steps

2001 –
Fluhrer
, Martin and Shamir found a class of RC4 keys called “weak keys”
If the first 2 bytes of enough key stream are known -> The RC4 key is discovered
The first 8 bytes of WEP packet is a known SNAP-SAP header
AirSnort
implements this attack
Recovers key after 20,000 packets = 11 seconds
Common Attacks: The FMS Attack

Does not recover the key, it just reveals the message
Exploits an ICV vulnerability
Steps:
Capture one packet
Truncate the last byte and try to guess one “value” for plaintext
Correct the checksum and send packet to AP
If guess is correct the AP will reply
Repeat until all bytes are decrypted

FA

Fragmentation means chopping a packet into smaller packets
Increases the traffic but improves performance in the presence of noise
802.11 and IP protocol allow fragmentation
802.11 allows for at most 16 fragments
This feature can be used for breaking WEP!
Common Attacks: The Fragmentation Attack

During fragmentation, WEP encryption is performed independently on each individual fragment using the same IV.
Possible to send multiple fragments (16) using the same key stream
Y
ou know more about the plaintext than you think you know.
Almost all encrypted data packets have a LLC/SNAP header, 8 bytes
XOR gives 8 bytes of the key stream
At this point, arbitrary data of (8 − 4) · 16 = 64 bytes (4 byte CRC tag) may be sent

Increase the number of bytes used for encryption (“protects” against FMS attack)
Remove the weak IV - keystream re-use vulnerabilities
Prevent key re-use
Extensible Authentication Protocol (EAP) – change often the WEP-key
Deploy Intrusion Detection Systems (IDS) to protect against injected traffic
Companies sell hardware using modified versions of the WEP protocol claiming to be secure
Protecting WEP

WEP has a long history of vulnerabilities and “fixes”
WEP is a good example of how attacks evolve and mature over time
Attacks that a few years ago took days, now take minutes if the right tools are used
2005 WEP is officially declared deprecated by IEEE 802.11 committee
2008 WEP used by 30% of users in a US university
Today – too many old networks, some using WEP
WEP must be abandoned once and for all, rather than patch it yet again !!!
Conclusions
